summary: Patrick McKenzie's Microconf 2012 talk covers systems-based marketing for software companies, aimed at engineers who'd rather build than sell. He argues that engineers have a natural advantage in building scalable marketing systems because they can exploit platforms like Google, Twitter, and app stores programmatically. The talk covers practical tactics including SaaS pricing strategy (charge more, segment by customer value, name plans carefully), automated marketing email sequences that educate then sell, and optimizing the first-run experience of software to dramatically improve activation rates. He also briefly discusses his consulting practice, where he commands $20,000/week by framing his work as revenue generation rather than hourly development labor.
key_points: A job turns time into money, but a business turns systems into money — engineers should build marketing systems, not do marketing manually

SaaS companies should charge dramatically more: the highest-priced plan typically generates 33-50% of revenue because enterprise buyers spend corporate budgets without pain, and cheaper plans attract pathological customers with disproportionate support costs

Email marketing is the highest-leverage channel for software companies — it's actually read by decision-makers, sits alongside important work in their inbox, and teachers literally wrote in asking for missing newsletters
The first five minutes of software use determines whether 40-60% of users ever return, so script that experience like the invasion of Normandy rather than dropping users on a blank screen
Validate business ideas by paying potential customers for their time and showing iPad mockups — if they don't say 'shut up and take my money,' you haven't found a real problem
money_quotes: A job is a system that turns time into money, and a business is a system that turns systems into money.
Ford might well be a company that sells cars, but T-Corp is a company that builds organizations that builds cars.
Charge more, charge more, charge more, charge more, charge more. Anyone have a question about pricing? You should charge more.
I'm a massage therapist. If my hands are on a telephone, they're not on someone's back, and if my hands aren't on someone's back, I'm not making money.
At 99 cents, people have very unreasonable expectations. 'What? This flashlight app didn't do my taxes! One stars!'
You raise prices, and you deal with less crazy people.
You script their first five minutes like it is the invasion of Normandy. 'You are going to do this, and then you are going to do this, and then you are going to be F-ing happy.'
If someone says, 'That's kind of interesting, tell me when that exists,' you have not successfully identified a problem that people actually have.
key_insight: Engineers have a unique advantage in business because they can build automated systems for marketing, sales, and customer activation — the companies that win are the ones that systematize everything from email sequences to first-run experiences rather than doing marketing manually.
